content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 9, 2017

Michael A. Guina
City Attorney
1333 Park Avenue
Emeryville, CA 94608-3517

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-137

Dear Mr. Guina:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTIONS

1. Is Service Employees International Union ("SEIU") Local 1021 a "related business entity" to SEIU-United Health Workers - West, as that term is defined in FPPC Regulation 18700.2?

2. Does Councilmember Medina's employment with United Health Workers-West give rise to a financial interest in a contract between the City of Emeryville and SEIU Local 1021, where SEIU- United Health Workers - West is not involved in the contract and does not represent any City employees?

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. As a nonprofit entity, SEIU is not a "business entity" as defined in Section 82005.

2. No. Under the facts presented, Councilmember Medina does not have a conflict of interest in a contract between the City and SEIU Local 1021 because there is no reasonably foreseeable financial effect on her employer, SEIU-United Health Workers-West.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-137
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the City of Emeryville, and you write on behalf of
Councilmember Ally Medina. Councilmember Medina is currently employed as the Bay Area
Regional Political Organizer with Service Employees International Union's United Workers West
("SEIU-UHW."). One of the City's bargaining groups is represented by a different local union of
SEIU, known as "SEIU-Local 1021." You request advice as to whether SEIU-Local 1021 is a
related business entity of SEIU-UHW; and whether Councilmember Medina's employment with
SEIU-UHW will pose a conflict of interest prohibiting her participation in future City labor and
employment deliberations and actions concerning SEIU-Local 1021.

Background: Service Employees International Union (SEIU)

SEIU is an advocacy organization (union) for working people. At the national level, it is an
organization of more than 2.1 million members, consisting of public, health care, building service,
office, professional, and industrial and allied workers. Its objectives include advancing and
strengthening the rights of its members through collective bargaining and helping Local Unions
"share experiences, pool resources" and learn from one another. SEIU is comprised of an unlimited
number of Local Unions, which are local branches chartered by SEIU. SEIU has jurisdiction over
Local Unions. SEIU derives funds from Local Unions by implementing taxes on the Local Unions.
Article XV of the SEIU Constitution sets forth duties for Local Unions, including setting forth the
minimum amount of dues that each Local Union must collect from its members. Members may
transfer from one Local Union to another. The SEIU Constitution provides: "No member of [SEIU]
shall injure the interests of another member by undermining such member in connection with wages
or financial status or by any other act, direct or indirect, which would wrongfully jeopardize a
member's office or standing."

SEIU holds regular conventions every four years. The convention consists of duly elected
delegates from all Local Unions in good standing. At the regular convention, the delegates elect the
officers of SEIU. SEIU is exempt pursuant to taxation pursuant to Section 501 (c)(5) of the Internal
Revenue Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 501 (c)(5).)

The City's Labor Groups

The City has approximately 234 employees. Those employees are divided into four
bargaining groups: Confidential, Administrative and Professional Employees ("CAMP"),
Emeryville Police Officers' Association ("POA"), Service Employees International Union LOCAL
1021 ("SEIU-Local 1021") and unrepresented employees. Currently, CAMP represents approximately 33 city
employees, or about 14% of the City's entire work force. POA represents approximately 34 city
employees, or about 14% of the City's entire work force. SEIU-Local 1021 represents
approximately 66 city employees, or about 2% of the City's entire work force. The group of
unrepresented employees range from Council-appointed positions, such as the City Manager and
City Attorney, to temporary and seasonal employees. There are 101 unrepresented employees.

SEIU-Local 1021 is an SEIU Local Union and subject to SEIU's Constitution and all
policies adopted pursuant to SEIU's Constitution. SEIU-Local 1021 derives its revenue from
initiations fees, dues, fines, assessments and other approved means. Dues paid by SEIU-Local 1021

File No. A-17-137
Page No. 3

members is based on a percentage of the member's wages, so the higher the member's wage, the
higher the member's dues.

Although the memorandum of understanding ("MOU") with each represented bargaining
group is negotiated separately, negotiations with one represented bargaining group can influence
negotiations with another. For example, the current CAMP MOU provides that if 1021 receives
certain base wage rate increases during the term of CAMP's MOU, then CAMP members may be
entitled to receive additional base wage rate increases as well. Because CAMP encompasses
positions that supervise SEIU-Local 1021 positions, and because the two groups are within the
same retirement system and have the same time period for terms, SEIU-Local 1021 negotiations are
more likely to influence CAMP negotiations (and vice versa), as opposed to POA negotiations.

SEIU-UHW: Councilmember Medina's Employer

On May 1, 2016, Councilmember Medina assumed the position of Bay Area Regional
Political Organizer with SEIU-UHW. Councilmember Medina receives a salary from SEIU-UHW
for providing her services as the Regional Political Organizer.² SEIU-UHW is a Local Union of
SEIU. As an employee of a Local Union, Councilmember Medina is entitled to participate in the
pension fund established by Article XX of the SEIU Constitution, and funded by the Local Unions.
SEIU-UHW has jurisdiction over healthcare workers. SEIU-UHW does not have jurisdiction over
any employees at the City of Emeryville because the City is not a provider of health care. In you
telephone call on June 6, 2017, you noted that SEIU-Local 1021 and SEIU-UHW are separate and
distinct legal entities.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit a public official from making, participating in
making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a governmental decision in
which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a "financial interest." (Section
87100.) A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87103
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or
on any one of five enumerated economic interests. (Section 87103.) Section 87103 defines the
interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. These interests are:

• A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or
more (Section 87103(a); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more.
(Section 87103(b).)

² Councilmember Medina was elected to the Emeryville City Council in the November 8, 2016 election. On
her assuming office Statement of Economic Interests, she disclosed the receipt of a salary of between $10,001-$100,000
from UHW.

File No. A-17-137
Page No. 4

• Any source of income, including promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• Any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts total $470 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).)

• His or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

Section 82005 defines a "business entity" as any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association. As a nonprofit entity, a union is not a "business entity" as defined in Section 82005. Therefore, Councilmember Medina does not have a financial interest in SEIU-UHW as a business entity under the Act.

You have indicated that Councilmember Medina is employed as the Bay Area Regional Political Organizer with SEIU-UHW, and that she receives a salary these services. As such, Councilmember Medina has an interest in SEIU-UHW as a source of income.

Foreseeability: A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is "explicitly involved" in the decision as a named party or subject of a governmental decision. (Regulation 18701.) Under your facts, Councilmember Medina's employer, SEIU-UHW is not explicitly involved in decisions involving SEIU-Local 1021, and Regulation 18701(b) provides following the foreseeability standard for decision where the financial interest is "not explicitly involved." "In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable."

In past advice letters, we have stated that where two local unions are affiliates under a broader union umbrella organization, so long as the two local unions are distinct non-profit entities, a public official does not have an interest in a governmental decision regarding the affiliated union. For example, in Barisone Advice Letter, No. 1-08-176, we stated:

You have explained that the two unions are distinct non-profit entities. While each Local is affiliated with the SEIU, all Local operating expenses including . employee salaries and benefits are provided through member dues. There is no cross-over in funding or decision-making between the ULTCW and the Local 521. We have previously stated that a public official does not have an economic interest in a governmental decision in situations where non-profits or unions are affiliated in this way. (See Rankin Advice Letter, A-94-310 and A-94-315.)

It is not reasonably foreseeable that decisions concerning represented bargaining Group in the City would have a financial effect on SEIU-UHW because local unions do not have financial interest in one another. As you noted, SEIU-Local 1021 and SEIU-UHW are separate and distinct

File No. A-17-137
Page No. 5

legal entities. Additionally, SEIU-UHW, which has jurisdiction over healthcare workers, does not
have jurisdiction over any City employees. Although the terms of one labor agreement to which the
City is a party may influence the other labor agreements that involve the City, SEIU-UHW does not
represent any City employees or negotiate any labor agreements with the City. Therefore,
Councilmember Medina does not have a financial interest in a contract between the City and SEIU-
Local 1021, so long as her employer SEIU-UHW is not involved in the contract and does not
represent any City employees.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By:    Zachary W. Norton
       Senior Counsel, Legal Division
